data.title: Rainmaker Worldwide Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Rainmaker Worldwide Inc., a company specializing in advanced water production and purification solutions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance amidst a challenging market environment and outlines significant business developments and strategic initiatives aimed at expanding its market presence and product offerings.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $34,250. The company experienced a significant decrease in revenue compared to the same period last year, reflecting a challenging market environment.
  • Gross Margin: $8,669. Despite the decline in revenue, the company maintained a positive gross margin, indicating effective cost management.
  • Loss from Operations: $(334,853). Operating losses increased due to higher general and administrative expenses.
  • Net Income (Loss): $(929,521). The net loss widened compared to the previous year, primarily driven by increased interest expenses and other financial costs.
  • Net Loss Per Share: $(0.0161). The loss per share decreased, reflecting the impact of share dilution from recent stock issuances.

Business Highlights

  • Geographical Performance: Rainmaker Worldwide Inc. continues to focus its efforts on Mexico and the United States, leveraging its strategic partnerships to expand its market presence in these regions. The company has established a Mexican subsidiary, RAKR México S.A. de C.V., to pursue commercial opportunities in Mexico related to water and renewable-technology business.
  • Sales Units: The company has generated limited revenue to date, with operations primarily focused on business development, market research, and technology research and development activities. As of September 30, 2025, the company had total assets of $104,189, indicating a focus on asset management and operational efficiency.
  • New Product Launches: Rainmaker Worldwide Inc. has expanded its product offerings through the acquisition of Miranda Environmental and Water Treatment Technologies. This acquisition has broadened the company's portfolio, particularly in the water treatment sector, allowing for increased revenue potential and shareholder value.
  • Future Outlook: The company remains committed to delivering advanced water production and purification solutions, leveraging its innovative technologies and expanded product range. Rainmaker aims to position itself as a leader in sustainable water solutions across North, Central, and South America, as well as the Caribbean.
  • Ongoing Approach to Sales and Marketing: The company's ongoing focus will be to pair Rainmaker technologies with those of Miranda and affiliate partners. Future sales will be heavily driven by independent distributors and project developers, with a strategic emphasis on expanding distribution rights and maximizing commercial potential.
  • RWI Acquires Miranda: On January 22, 2024, RWI finalized the acquisition of Miranda, significantly broadening its product offerings and distribution capabilities. This strategic move is expected to enhance RWI's market position and drive future growth.
  • Recent Developments: The formation of RAKR México S.A. de C.V. with a local partner marks a significant step in Rainmaker's strategy to capitalize on opportunities in the Mexican market. This joint venture is expected to enhance the company's regional presence and operational capabilities.

SEC Filing: Rainmaker Worldwide Inc. [ RAKR ] - 10-Q - Nov. 14, 2025

data.title: MILLS MUSIC TRUST SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Mills Music Trust, a company that derives its revenue from royalty income generated by its music catalogue, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter. The report provides insights into the company's financial and operational performance, including a significant settlement agreement with EMI and plans for future distributions to Unit Holders.

Financial Highlights

The Form 10-Q report did not provide specific financial metrics such as Revenue, Gross Profit, Operating Income, Net Income, or Net Income Per Share. The focus was primarily on the operational aspects and the settlement agreement with EMI.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments

The Trust's revenue is derived from the Contingent Portion payments based on royalty income generated by the music catalogue. These payments are made quarterly by EMI and are distributed to Unit Holders after covering expenses and liabilities.

Geographical Performance

The music catalogue generates royalty income both domestically and internationally. EMI, as the administrator, is responsible for collecting royalties from various regions, which impacts the Contingent Portion payments to the Trust.

Operational Challenges

The Trust faces uncertainties in generating long-term royalty income due to factors such as changes in copyright laws, the expiration of copyright protection, and ongoing disputes regarding the calculation of the Contingent Portion.

Contingent Portion Payment Dispute

There is an ongoing dispute with EMI regarding the calculation method of the Contingent Portion payments. The Trust believes the payments should be a fixed 75% of gross royalty income, but EMI disputes this interpretation.

Settlement Agreement

A settlement agreement was reached with EMI to resolve claims related to underpayments up to December 31, 2024. EMI agreed to a $500,000 settlement payment and amendments to the Asset Purchase Agreement to cap certain deductions.

Future Outlook

The Trust plans to release reserved cash balances and make distributions to Unit Holders following the receipt of the settlement payment. The Trust will continue to assess the need for reserves for future administrative expenses.

Administrative Expenses

The Trust incurs administrative expenses related to third-party service providers and trustee fees, which are deducted from the Contingent Portion payments before distribution to Unit Holders.

Segment Information

The Trust operates in a single segment, focusing on receiving and distributing Contingent Portion payments. The key performance metric is cash distributions per Trust Unit.

SEC Filing: MILLS MUSIC TRUST [ MMTRS ] - 10-Q - Nov. 14, 2025

data.title: Moody's Upgrades Its Outlook on Banks But Flags Deregulation as Risk — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Rebecca Ungarino

Moody's Ratings now has a rosier outlook on the U.S. banking system over the next 12 to 18 months because the firm expects a mix of lower interest rates and "muted but stable" economic growth to help lenders' profitability and asset quality.

The ratings firm lifted its outlook for the economically sensitive sector to stable from negative while noting it expects real gross domestic product growth in the U.S. to slow to 1.8% in 2026 from 2% this year.

"The stable outlook also incorporates an expectation that a modest pickup in loan growth and a steepening yield curve will enhance net interest income, most banks' primary revenue source," Moody's said Thursday.

For Moody's, a shift in outlook is different from a change in rating, which remains the same, at Aa1 stable. The latest quarter of earnings from large banks, including JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo and Bank of America, showed solid or improving consumer and commercial credit, with generally upbeat comments from executives on the economy.

At the same time, the Moody's said that expectations of a lighter touch from financial regulators adds some risk to the banking sector.

"The prospect of a loosening of bank regulation and supervision adds some incremental risk for bank creditors," Moody's wrote. "Greater clarity on bank capital rules and stress testing will likely lead to lower capital ratios at the largest banks."

This is a developing story. Please check back for updates.

Write to Rebecca Ungarino at rebecca.ungarino@barrons.com

data.title: US Postal Service seeks reforms as it reports $9 billion yearly loss
data.description:
By David Shepardson

The U.S. Postal Service said on Friday it was seeking new administrative and legislative reforms as it reported a $9 billion yearly loss, slightly wider than the loss in the prior fiscal year.

New Postmaster General David Steiner said USPS must be more efficient and that it still has a "significant systemic annual revenue and cost imbalance." He added: "To correct our financial imbalances, we must explore new revenue opportunities and public policy changes to improve our business model."

USPS, which lost $9.5 billion in the prior year, has lost more than $100 billion since 2007 despite significant restructuring and legislative reforms. The U.S. Congress in 2022 provided the Postal Service with about $50 billion in financial relief over a decade.

USPS is seeking reforms including changes to retiree pension benefit funding rules, diversification of pension assets, raising the statutory debt ceiling, and workers’ compensation administration reform.

In February, Republican President Donald Trump called USPS a "tremendous loser for this country," and said he was considering merging the Postal Service with the U.S. Commerce Department, a move Democrats said would violate federal law.

Under White House pressure, the previous USPS chief, Louis DeJoy, resigned in March. He was one of many officials forced out under Trump.

DeJoy led efforts to drastically restructure the money-losing USPS for nearly five years, including cutting forecast cumulative losses over a decade to $80 billion from $160 billion even as mail volumes fell to the lowest level since 1968.

The price of first-class mail stamps rose earlier this year to 78 cents from 73 cents but USPS in September opted not to raise prices in January. Stamp prices are up 46% since early 2019, when they were 50 cents.

The USPS, an agency with 635,000 employees, reduced its workforce by 10,000 workers earlier this year through a voluntary retirement program.

data.title: REG - Management Cons.Grp - Rule 2.4 Takeover Code Announcement
data.description:
RNS Number : 6672H Management Consulting Group PLC 14 November 2025  

NOT FOR RELEASE, PUBLICATION OR DISTRIBUTION, IN WHOLE OR IN PART, DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY, IN, INTO OR FROM ANY JURISDICTION WHERE TO DO SO WOULD CONSTITUTE A VIOLATION OF THE RELEVANT LAWS OR REGULATIONS OF SUCH JURISDICTION 

Management Consulting Group (the "Company")

This is an announcement made under Rule 2.4 of the City Code on Takeovers and Mergers (the "Takeover Code") and is not an announcement of a firm intention by any party to make an oﬀer under Rule 2.7 of the Takeover Code. Further to an ongoing review by the board of directors of the Company (the "Board") of the various strategic options available to the Company to enable it to continue and accelerate the growth of its business, the Board has unanimously concluded that it would be appropriate to investigate the sale of the Company and therefore has now decided to commence a "Formal Sale Process" for the Company (as referred to in Note 2 on Rule 2.6 of the Takeover Code) (the "Formal Sale Process"). As part of the Formal Sale Process, the Company has contacted a number of potential counterparties with a view to obtaining expressions of interest.

The Takeover Panel has granted a dispensation from the requirements of Rules 2.4(a), 2.4(b) and 2.6(a) of the Code such that any interested party participating in the Formal Sale Process will not be required to be publicly identified as a result of this announcement (subject to note 3 to Rule 2.2 of the Code) and will not be subject to the 28 day deadline referred to in Rule 2.6(a) of the Code, for so long as it is participating in the Formal Sale Process.

Parties with a potential interest in participating in the Formal Sale Process should contact Equiteq at jerome.glynn-smith@equiteq.com or on +44 7415 534 557 as soon as possible. Any party participating in the Formal Sale Process will be required, at a minimum, to enter into a non-disclosure agreement on terms satisfactory to the Company and to provide certain information to the Company and its advisers. The Company reserves the right to alter, terminate or adjourn the Formal Sale Process at any time and in such cases will make an appropriate announcement.

There can be no certainty that an oﬀer will be made for the Company, nor as to the terms on which any transaction might be pursued.

Further information on the status of the Formal Sale Process will be provided when appropriate.

The Board has appointed Equiteq Corporate Finance Limited ("Equiteq") as its Financial Adviser with regards to the Formal Sale Process. Equiteq Corporate Finance Limited is an Appointed Representative of Frank Investments Limited, who is authorised and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (FRN627697) in the United Kingdom. Equiteq is acting exclusively for the Company and for no-one else in connection with the matters set out in this announcement and will not be responsible to anyone other than the Company for providing the protections afforded to clients of Equiteq or for providing advice in relation to any matter referred to herein. Neither Equiteq nor any of its affiliates nor any of their respective directors, officers or employees owes or accepts any duty, liability or responsibility whatsoever to any person who is not a client of Equiteq in connection with this announcement or any matter referred to herein.

Under Rule 8.3(a) of the Code, any person who is interested in 1% or more of any class of relevant securities of an offeree company or of any securities exchange offeror (being any offeror other than an offeror in respect of which it has been announced that its offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash) must make an Opening Position Disclosure following the commencement of the offer period and, if later, following the announcement in which any securities exchange offeror is first identified. An Opening Position Disclosure must contain details of the person's interests and short positions in, and rights to subscribe for, any relevant securities of each of (i) the offeree company and (ii) any securities exchange offeror(s). An Opening Position Disclosure by a person to whom Rule 8.3(a) applies must be made by no later than 3.30 pm (London time) on the 10th business day following the commencement of the offer period and, if appropriate, by no later than 3.30 pm (London time) on the 10th business day following the announcement in which any securities exchange offeror is first identified. Relevant persons who deal in the relevant securities of the offeree company or of a securities exchange offeror prior to the deadline for making an Opening Position Disclosure must instead make a Dealing Disclosure.

Under Rule 8.3(b) of the Code, any person who is, or becomes, interested in 1% or more of any class of relevant securities of the offeree company or of any securities exchange offeror must make a Dealing Disclosure if the person deals in any relevant securities of the offeree company or of any securities exchange offeror. A Dealing Disclosure must contain details of the dealing concerned and of the person's interests and short positions in, and rights to subscribe for, any relevant securities of each of (i) the offeree company and (ii) any securities exchange offeror(s), save to the extent that these details have previously been disclosed under Rule 8. A Dealing Disclosure by a person to whom Rule 8.3(b) applies must be made by no later than 3.30 pm (London time) on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

If two or more persons act together pursuant to an agreement or understanding, whether formal or informal, to acquire or control an interest in relevant securities of an offeree company or a securities exchange offeror, they will be deemed to be a single person for the purpose of Rule 8.3.

Opening Position Disclosures must also be made by the offeree company and by any offeror and Dealing Disclosures must also be made by the offeree company, by any offeror and by any persons acting in concert with any of them (see Rules 8.1, 8.2 and 8.4).

The Panel has also granted a dispensation from the requirement in Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to an RIS. Accordingly, any Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the Company by email to dwhitehead@proudfoot.com and will be published on the Company's website at www.mcgplc.com. A copy must also be sent to the Panel's Market Surveillance Unit by email to monitoring@thetakeoverpanel.org.uk.

data.title: Fitch Rates Metropolitan Water District of Southern CA Var Rate Water Revs 'AA+'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Austin-14 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has assigned a 'AA+' long-term rating to the following bonds issued by the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California (MWD or the district):

--Approximately $184.2 million special variable rate water revenue refunding bonds, series 2025B (including bank bonds).

The proceeds will be used to refund a portion of the district's outstanding senior parity obligations. The bonds will initially bear interest in the weekly mode with interest payments due monthly and final maturity in 2047. Bondholders have the right to tender the bonds on any business day. A standby bond purchase agreement with PNC Bank, National Association supports the purchase of tendered bonds in the event of a failed remarketing of the bonds. Fitch expects to assign a short-term rating to the bonds based on PNC Bank, National Association.

In addition, Fitch has affirmed the following ratings:

--Approximately $3 billion outstanding senior lien and subordinate lien water revenue bonds and term bonds at 'AA+';

-- Bank bond ratings associated with water revenue bonds, series 2016B-2, 2017A, 2021A, 2022C-2, 2024B-1 and 2024D at 'AA+';

--Approximately $173.2 million water bank revenue bonds issued by the Antelope Valley-East Kern (AVEK) Water Agency Financing Authority (the authority) on behalf of the district at 'AA+';

--The district's Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'AA+';

--Approximately $17.2 million waterworks general obligation (GO) refunding bonds at 'AAA'.

The Rating Outlook for all the ratings is Stable.

The 'AA+' IDR and revenue bond ratings reflect MWD's historically very low leverage, measured as net adjusted debt to adjusted funds available for debt service (FADS), within the context of the district's very strong revenue defensibility and operating risk profiles, both assessed at 'aa'. Between fiscal 2018 (FYE June 30) and fiscal 2022, leverage ranged between 5.4x and 6.5x. However, lower demand due to drought conservation measures weakened FADS and overall performance in fiscal 2023 and drove leverage up to 8.2x. Leverage increased further in fiscal 2024, largely because of sustained lower demand as purchasers used more of their respective local supplies rather than purchasing from MWD.

MWD's board of directors (the board) increased both water rates and property tax rates in the current biennial budget covering 2025 and 2026. Overall rate increases of 8.5% were adopted for both 2025 and 2026 calendar years and, effective for fiscal 2025, the property tax rate was doubled. As a result of increased revenues, expected results for fiscal 2025 reflect leverage likely closer to 5.0x.

Fitch's Analytical Stress Test (FAST) reflects leverage below 5.0x in most years of the rating case. However, Fitch's analysis does not include the full costs related to the Pure Water Southern California (PWSC) initiative as the board has not approved full-scale implementation. To date, costs related to a demonstration project and environmental planning have been incurred as operating costs.

MWD recently updated design and construction cost estimates for phase I of PWSC to $9.4 billion (in 2025 dollars, including contingencies) from $6.4 billion (in 2023 dollars). Other partner agencies would likely share in the costs, but the magnitude of potential costs and debt for the district could be meaningful. As such, when assessing the financial profile at 'aa', more consideration is given to the potential increase in leverage related to PWSC rather than the exceptionally low leverage currently projected.

MWD's revenue defensibility is supported by the district's very strong purchaser credit quality (PCQ), independent rate-raising ability, and unlimited ability to reallocate costs (pursuant to Fitch's criteria). The district's business model is subject to price competitiveness relative to local sources. However, while MWD's supplies in many cases are the most expensive, the significant inherent demand for the district's water supply cannot be reasonably replaced.

The district manages sales volatility by maintaining a strong financial cushion and robust liquidity. MWD's operating risk assessment is supported by the district's very low operating cost burden, coupled with its low life cycle ratio and moderate investment needs.

General Obligation Rating Differential

The 'AAA' rating on the district's GO bonds, which is one notch above MWD's 'AA+' IDR, is based on the bonds' statutory lien of tax revenue, the specific provisions of the California legislation granting such lien, and the application of Fitch's criteria. Fitch believes the statute provides bondholders with a substantial preferential right in a bankruptcy proceeding, warranting a GO bond rating up to two notches higher than the entity's IDR pursuant to Fitch's rating criteria. Given MWD's 'AA+' IDR, only a one-notch uplift is possible at this time.

On July 13, 2015, the California legislature passed Senate Bill 222 and the governor signed it into law. The bill amended section 53515 of the California Government Code and section 15251 of the Education Code to provide that a statutory lien automatically arises on all pledged revenues securing GO bonds issued by local agencies or school districts.

SECURITY

The series 2025B bonds are payable from and secured solely by a pledge of and a senior lien on MWD's net water revenues. The district has other outstanding senior lien revenue bonds and also has outstanding subordinate lien water revenue bonds, which are payable from MWD's net water revenues junior in payment to the bonds being sold.

The water bank revenue bonds are secured by installment payments to the authority from the district on a subordinate lien on MWD's net water revenues. MWD's obligation to pay the installment payments securing the water bank revenue bonds is on parity with MWD's outstanding subordinate water revenue bonds and incurred under the district's master subordinate bond resolution.

The GO bonds are general obligation indebtedness of the district, payable from unlimited ad valorem taxes on all taxable property in the district.

KEY RATING DRIVERS Revenue Defensibility - 'aa'

Ability to Reallocate Costs; Very Strong PCQ

The district retains independent rate-setting ability and the full ability to reallocate costs (as considered under Fitch's criteria), although the district is subject to a higher degree of competitive pressure than other wholesalers. While MWD's members all pay fixed readiness-to-serve charges, which typically accounts for around 8% of revenues, they are not required to buy minimum amounts of water from MWD (aside from stipulations for San Diego County Water Authority [SDCWA] under its amended and restated exchange agreement); instead, members often use the imported water supply to supplement their other sources.

MWD's PCQ is very strong, with its Fitch's Purchaser Credit Index at less than 1.5. The PCQ is assessed based on the credit quality of the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power (water revenue bonds rated AA-/Stable; 17% of MWD revenues in fiscal 2025), SDCWA (IDR AA+/Stable; 15%), Eastern Municipal Water District (IDR AAA/Stable; 9%), and Western Municipal Water District (IDR AAA/Stable; 6%).

Operating Risk - 'aa'

Very Low Operating Cost Burden

In fiscal 2024, the district's operating cost burden was very low at around $4,150 per million gallons (mg) of water transactions, reflective of the significant economies of scale it achieves in providing wholesale services across a territory encompassing about 5,200 square miles. The lower demand in fiscal 2023 and fiscal 2024 drove the ratio higher than years prior when it measured less than $3,000 per mg. Based on fiscal 2025 results and demand, the cost burden is expected to be closer to $3,500 per mg.

The lifecycle ratio is typically around 45%. Capex increased in recent years and is expected to have outpaced depreciation expenses in fiscal 2025, as it did in fiscal 2024. Long-range capital planning remains robust, but the lifecycle ratio could gradually increase if planned capital spending is not fully executed. Conversely, if the district moves forward with PWSC, capital spending could increase notably as soon as fiscal 2027 which would likely drive the ratio lower.

MWD's capital improvement plan from fiscal years 2026-2030, excluding design and construction costs associated with PWSC, totals $1.76 billion. Debt financing, including remaining proceeds from prior issuances, is expected to fund roughly 40% of the capital plan while the balance is largely expected to be funded on a pay-go basis. Some grant monies may also provide funding, as available. The capital program is focused on ongoing renewal and replacement of assets, along with compliance of drinking water regulations.

Two large-scale projects could potentially impact capital costs over the intermediate to longer term. The first of these, PWSC, could begin to see costs ramp up in fiscal 2027, but any participation beyond fiscal 2026 would require board approval. Updated estimates were presented to the board in September 2025 reflecting costs of $9.4 billion, the equivalent of a 47% increase from a previous 2023 estimate. Fitch has not included these capital costs in its analysis since offsetting revenue adjustments, project participants, and the potential cost-sharing agreements have not been finalized. Fitch will continue to monitor the progress of PWSC and its potential impact on capital and/or financial performance.

The second, the Delta conveyance project, has a longer ramp-up period for costs. The state's Department of Water Resources (DWR) released an updated cost estimate of $20.1 billion in May 2024, up from $15.9 billion in 2020. To date, MWD has participated and shared costs related to planning and pre-construction only. MWD's participation and investment over the longer term would also require board approval. Fitch will also continue to assess whether this project could potentially impact MWD's performance once the full scope of costs and participants is better defined.

Financial Profile - 'aa'

Leverage Returns to Historical Levels, Neutral Liquidity

Leverage between fiscal 2018 and fiscal 2022 was consistently between 5.4x and 6.5x. Over this period, leverage peaked at 6.5x in fiscal 2018 and was at its lowest level at 5.4x in fiscal 2022. As noted, performance weakened in fiscal 2023 and fiscal 2024, with leverage exceeding 8.0x in both years on lower demand in consecutive years. With higher sales and tax revenue expected in fiscal 2025 leverage is likely to measure close to 5.0x for the year.

The liquidity profile is neutral to the assessment, but coverage dropped in fiscal 2023 on weaker margins. Including the $153 million transfer from the rate stabilization fund (RSF) in fiscal 2023, Fitch-calculated coverage of full obligations (COFO), which includes the fixed services expense, was 1.0x. MWD remained in compliance with debt service coverage (DSC) covenants; as disclosed by the district, DSC was 1.48x and 1.14x in fiscal years 2023 and 2024, respectively. The district's bond covenant requires 1.0x coverage. Current days cash on hand measured 111 days in fiscal 2024. As most of a $400 million revolving credit facility was utilized as of FYE 2024, the liquidity cushion was nearly the same at 115 days.

The FAST considers the potential trend of key ratios in a base case and stress case over a five-year period. The stress case is designed to impose capital costs 10% above expected base case levels and evaluate potential variability in projected key ratios. The FAST reflects Fitch's view of a reasonable scenario, which is generally informed by publicly available and/or management provided information with respect to capital expenditures, user charges and rate of revenue and expenditure growth.

In the base case scenario, leverage could measure 6.0x in fiscal 2026 but declines annually thereafter, reaching 3.5x by fiscal 2030. The stress case, which is considered the rating case, peaks at 6.1x in both fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027 before declining in each of the following years. The 'aa' financial profile assessment reflects not only historical performance but also considers the higher leverage that would likely be realized should the district move forward with PWSC. The liquidity profile is expected to remain neutral to the assessment over the five-year horizon.

Asymmetric Additional Risk Considerations

No asymmetric additive risk considerations affected this rating determination.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Sustained leverage around 8.0x or above in Fitch's rating case, whether as a result of significantly lower water transactions or larger-than-anticipated capital projects;

--Actions at the federal level in response to the Colorado River drought that materially affect how the district manages its supply portfolio and/or operations;

--Capital spending that significantly lags annual depreciation and averages less than 80% on a sustained basis could weaken the operating risk assessment;

--Significant weakening in the PCQ that erodes the revenue defensibility assessment.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Greater clarity on large capital projects, specifically PWSC and the Delta Conveyance project, would be needed prior to any positive rating action;

--Sustained actual and projected leverage below 5.0x in Fitch's rating case, assuming stability in the revenue defensibility and operating risk profiles.

PROFILE

MWD is a wholesale water supplier to 26 agencies in Southern California, many of which have some form of local water supply. The total population served by the district, either wholly or in part, is estimated at nearly 19 million. The district's service area includes approximately 85% of the population within the six-county area comprised of Los Angeles, Orange, Riverside, San Bernardino, San Diego, and Ventura counties. The six-county area is the most densely populated and heavily industrialized portion of Southern California and accounts for about 55% of the state's population.

The district's water supply is primarily derived from two sources: Northern California's Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta (the Delta) through a long-term contract with the DWR for water from the State Water Project (the SWP), and the Colorado River. The SWP is owned by the state and operated by DWR. The project transports Feather River water released from Oroville Dam and unregulated flows diverted directly from the Delta via a 444-mile aqueduct (the California Aqueduct) to MWD's service territory. MWD is the largest of the 29 agencies having a SWP water contract with DWR, both in terms of service area population and water allocation (at about 46%).

MWD's contract with DWR allocates the district slightly more than 1.9 million acre-feet (maf) per year, although the actual average delivery to contracted agencies is often lower than the full allocation driven by water conditions in the Sierra Mountains. Furthermore, DWR alters the operations of the SWP to accommodate requirements to protect various species of endangered fish, which can limit actual water available for delivery to purchasing agencies.

DWR announces initial allocation levels in the November/December time frame for the following calendar year and can subsequently adjust the allocation during the year based on hydrological conditions. For the first time since 2006 the final allocation for 2023 was set at 100% in light of the significant rain and snow events last year. For the 2025 water year (ended Sept. 30) DWR implemented a 50% allocation of SWP water. (No allocation for the 2026 water year has been announced as of the time of this publication.)

MWD has a fourth priority right to the Colorado River water, totaling 550,000 acre-feet (af) per year, which is the fourth and last-ranked priority right under California's 4.4 maf per year firm allocation. The district also holds a fifth priority right to 662,000 af per year in excess of the state's allotment. However, receipt of the fifth priority right depends on water conditions, and the Colorado River system has experienced a drought since 2000. Due to the severity of drought conditions along the Colorado River, in August 2021, the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation (the bureau) announced the first-ever water shortage in the Colorado River Basin, which had the most immediate impact on Arizona.

In May 2023, California, Nevada, and Arizona reached a consensus proposal that would reduce deliveries to the three states by 2.3 maf through 2026 and agreed to an additional reduction of 700,000 af. The bureau announced its projection that the Tier 1 shortage would remain in effect for 2025 and 2026.

All seven of the Colorado River basin states and Mexico will need to renegotiate the river compact that expires at the end of 2026 to establish delivery amounts beyond that date. To date, a separate proposal has been submitted by both the Upper Basin states (Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, and Wyoming) and the Lower Basin states (Nevada, Arizona, and California), but as of the time of publication no consensus has been reached among all seven of the basin states. Fitch will continue to monitor the drought situation to assess whether it becomes a more immediate credit concern for the district.

In pursuit of additional storage capacity, the district entered into an agreement with AVEK Water Agency to establish a groundwater banking program known as the High Desert Water Bank Program. Construction costs were financed by the authority with MWD as the sole obligor for repayment of the water bank revenue bonds. Upon expected construction completion in 2027, the maximum storage capacity for MWD supplies is expected to be 280,000 af. At the district's discretion, up to 70,000 af of stored water could be returned annually to the district.

Updated U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Regulations

MWD does not expect any immediate operational impact due to the EPA's recently finalized rule in relation to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS). Testing to date has not detected any of the regulated compounds at levels exceeding the limits set by the EPA. Compliance with the EPA's Lead and Copper Rule is not an issue since, as a wholesale provider, lead service lines (LSL) are not within MWD's oversight.

Sources of Information

In addition to the sources of information identified in Fitch's applicable criteria specified below, this action was informed by data from DIVER by Solve.

A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. data.title: SMX Announces Effective Date of Reverse Stock Split
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / November 14, 2025 / SMX (Security Matters) Public Limited Company (the "Company"), today announced that the reverse stock split of the Company's ordinary shares will begin trading on an adjusted basis giving effect to the reverse stock split on November 18, 2025 under the existing ticker symbol "SMX". The new CUSIP number of the Company's ordinary shares will be G8267K307 and the new ISIN code will be IE000UPDVNX9.

On May 2, 2025, the Company's Shareholders approved a proposal to amend the Company's constitution to allow the Company's Board of Director's to consolidate and/or divide all or any of the Company's classes of shares as the Board of Directors sees fit. As such, Shareholder approval was not required to effect the reverse stock split.

The Company's Board of Directors' fixed the split ratio at 8:1, every 8 ordinary shares of the Company with a nominal value of $0.00000000000312817946 per share will be automatically combined into one (1) ordinary share with a nominal value of $0.00000000002502543568 per share. This will reduce the number of outstanding ordinary shares of the Company from 8,404,581 pre-reverse stock split to 1,050,572 post-reverse stock split.

Outstanding Company options, warrants and other applicable convertible securities, including the Company's warrants listed on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the symbol SMXWW which will retain its existing CUSIP number, will be proportionately adjusted in accordance with their respective terms. No fractional shares will be issued in connection with the reverse stock split. Instead, the Company will aggregate the fractional entitlements of shareholders who otherwise would be entitled to receive fractional shares because they hold a number of ordinary shares not evenly divisible by 8ordinary shares pursuant to the reverse stock split or they hold less than the number of ordinary shares which should be consolidated into one ordinary share pursuant to the reverse stock split and, to the extent possible, sell such aggregated fractional ordinary shares on the basis of prevailing market prices at such time.

Continental Stock Transfer & Trust Company is acting as exchange agent for the reverse stock split and will send instructions to any shareholders of record who hold stock certificates regarding the exchange of certificates. Shareholders with shares held in book-entry form or through a bank, broker, or other nominee are not required to take any action and will see the impact of the reverse stock split reflected in their accounts on or after November 19, 2025. Such beneficial holders may contact their bank, broker, or nominee for more information. Continental Stock Transfer may be reached for questions at (212) 509-4000.

-Ends-

For further information contact:

SMX GENERAL ENQUIRIES

E: info@securitymattersltd.com

About SMX

As global businesses face new and complex challenges relating to carbon neutrality and meeting new governmental and regional regulations and standards, SMX is able to offer players along the value chain access to its marking, tracking, measuring and digital platform technology to transition more successfully to a low-carbon economy.

Forward-Looking Statements

data.title: ICE Midday: Canola Still on the Rise
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange extended their rally on Friday.

An analyst said if the January contract can stay above C$650 per tonne throughout the entire session, it would begin to establish a "base of resistance". However, he also believes the contract to be slightly overbought.

The Canadian Grain Commission reported canola exports for the week ended Nov. 9 were 121,200 tonnes, down from the previous week's 188,400. The year-to-date total reached 1.54 million tonnes, compared to 3.36 million the same time last year.

Chicago soyoil was up, European rapeseed was down and Malaysian palm oil was mixed. Crude oil jumped by more than US$1 per barrel after Ukrainian drones struck a major Russian oil depot last night.

The Canadian dollar was steady compared to Thursday's close.

The United States Department of Agriculture will release its November supply and demand estimates at 11 a.m. CST after its October report was cancelled due to the U.S. government shutdown.

About 23,300 canola contracts have traded at 10:12 CST. Prices in Canadian dollars per metric tonne:

Notes to Editors:

Futura Medical plc (AIM: FUM) is the developer of innovative sexual health products, including lead product Eroxon® and products WSD4000 and Eroxon® Intense. Our core strength lies in our research, development and commercialisation of topically delivered gel formulations in sexual health products.

Sexual health issues are prevalent in both men and women. ED impacts 1 in 5 men globally across all adult age brackets, with approximately half of all men over 40 experiencing ED and 25% of all new diagnoses being in men under 40. Around 60% of women experience at least one symptom of sexual dysfunction, and only one in four women seek professional help, and remain chronically underserved.

Eroxon®, Futura's clinically proven lead product, has been developed for the treatment of Erectile Dysfunction ("ED"). The highly differentiated product, which is the only topical gel treatment for ED available over the counter and helps men get an erection in ten minutes, addresses significant unmet needs in the ED market. Eroxon® has been nominated for a number of healthcare industry awards and has won two to-date.

Futura has distribution partners in place in a number of major consumer markets including Haleon in the US, the largest market for ED in the world, and Cooper Consumer Health in Europe.

WSD4000 is a topical treatment designed for the symptoms of impaired sexual response and function in women. There is currently no regulatory approved OTC treatment available for impaired sexual response and function in women. WSD4000 has the potential to be an effective, breakthrough treatment for the common symptoms associated with impaired sexual response and function, such as lack of desire, arousal and lubrication.

data.title: Ribbon Acquisition Corp. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Ribbon Acquisition Corp., a blank check company focused on mergers and acquisitions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report provides a detailed overview of the company's financial performance and operational activities, highlighting significant developments and future plans.

Financial Highlights

Net Income: Ribbon Acquisition Corp. reported a net income of $62,142 for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $569,295 for the nine months ended September 30, 2025. This income was primarily earned from marketable securities held in the Trust Account.

Net Income Per Ordinary Share, Redeemable Ordinary Shares: The net income per redeemable ordinary share was $0.08 for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $0.31 for the nine months ended September 30, 2025.

Net Loss Per Ordinary Share, Non-Redeemable Ordinary Shares: The net loss per non-redeemable ordinary share was $(0.23) for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $(0.61) for the nine months ended September 30, 2025.

Operating Expenses: The company incurred operating expenses of $463,712 for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $892,007 for the nine months ended September 30, 2025. These expenses were primarily due to administrative fees.

Income Earned on Marketable Securities Held in Trust Account: The income earned on marketable securities held in the Trust Account was $525,854 for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $1,461,302 for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, contributing significantly to the net income.

Business Highlights

Business Combination Agreement: Ribbon Acquisition Corp. entered into a Business Combination Agreement with DRC Medicine Inc., DRC Medicine Ltd., and DRC Merger Inc. This agreement involves a share exchange and a merger, with the aim of DRC Medicine becoming a wholly-owned subsidiary of a new holding company, PubCo.

Operational Focus: The company is a blank check company, primarily focused on effecting a merger, share exchange, asset acquisition, stock purchase, reorganization, or similar business combination with one or more businesses.

Geographical Performance: The business combination involves entities in the United States and Japan, indicating a focus on expanding operations across these regions.

Future Outlook: The company plans to complete its initial business combination by January 16, 2026. If not completed, the company will undergo liquidation. The management is focused on using the proceeds from the IPO and private placement to finance the business combination and subsequent operations.

Segment Reporting: The company operates as a single reportable segment, with the Chief Executive Officer reviewing the overall performance to allocate resources and assess financial performance.

Administrative Support Services: Ribbon Acquisition Corp. has an agreement to pay $10,000 per month for office space, utilities, and administrative support, which will continue until the completion of the business combination or liquidation.

Recent Developments: The company filed a Schedule 13G indicating that RiverNorth Capital Management, LLC owns approximately 6.18% of Ribbon’s outstanding units, reflecting significant investor interest.

Operational Challenges: The company faces a working capital deficit and has incurred significant costs in pursuit of a business combination, raising concerns about its ability to continue as a going concern if the business combination is not completed within the specified timeframe.

SEC Filing: Ribbon Acquisition Corp. [ RIBB ] - 10-Q - Nov. 14, 2025
